The C.I.A.'s Reason for Living
nytimes_articles.abstract:
In an effort to strengthen the battered Central Intelligence Agency, a Presidential commission has recently recommended steps that would do just the opposite -- weaken it as an independent and objective interpreter of foreign events. 

     The commission recommends centralizing intelligence analysis in policy agencies like the Department of Defense, and it suggests creating a new National Imagery and Mapping Agency, to work as a "combat support agency." The C.I.A.'s National Photographic Interpretation Center would be abolished; analysis of all  satellite photography would be done by the Pentagon.

nytimes_articles.headline: Corrections
New Jersey's Tax Cuts Have Worked
nytimes_articles.abstract:
States around the country are cutting taxes, and no state has received more attention than New Jersey, where Gov. Christine Todd Whitman's income tax cuts have made her a rising Republican star. But because all states pass most of their revenues back to local governments, critics say that states' cuts will force local property taxes to rise -- leaving taxpayers no better off. 

     A study we have done on New Jersey's taxes for the Manhattan Institute indicates that such criticism is unjustified. We found that in the majority of towns and cities, property taxes have not risen since Governor Whitman's cuts took effect. Even in the municipalities that have raised taxes, property owners  are still saving money and will continue to do so.

Mounting Legal Assault Against 'Megan's Law'
A Performer Is Not a Teacher
nytimes_articles.abstract:
After years of desperation and neglect, the arts education community in New York is ecstatic. The Annenberg Foundation has announced a grant of $12 million, which with matching funds would bring a $36 million, five-year arts program into the New York City public schools. But the plan that a consulting group, Artsvision, has developed for the grant money is misguided and should be reconsidered. 

     To make a real difference in children's lives, a public school arts program must provide regular, sequential training by skilled and experienced teachers. Artsvision acknowledges the importance of instruction but is willing to leave the choice of instructors, and what they will teach, to outside groups: "Individual schools, or groups of schools will form partnerships with arts organizations, community-based organizations, artists or colleges," it says. Classes would be run by outside performers and artists, untrained classroom teachers or parents -- not by trained arts teachers.
